name: ClickHouse
what_it_does: ClickHouse is a blazing-fast open-source columnar OLAP database designed for real-time analytics over massive datasets. It stores data in column-oriented format with aggressive compression, enabling queries that scan billions of rows per second. ClickHouse accepts SQL via an HTTP interface (port 8123/8443) and a native binary protocol (port 9440), with support for many input/output formats (JSON, CSV, Parquet, Arrow). ClickHouse Cloud is the managed offering with a REST Management API for programmatic cluster management. The HTTP interface supports streaming responses, progress headers, and session-based queries — making it well-suited for agents that need to run complex analytical queries.
use_cases: Sub-second analytical queries over billions of rows of event, log, or telemetry data
Real-time dashboards and alerting over streaming event data ingested at millions of events/second
Product analytics and user behavior analysis with complex funnel and session queries
Log analytics and observability data storage with time-based partitioning and TTL policies
Programmatic ClickHouse Cloud service provisioning and scaling via Management API
Data warehouse queries using S3-backed external table integrations (ClickHouse reads directly from S3/GCS)
not_for: OLTP transactional workloads requiring row-level updates, foreign keys, or ACID across multiple tables (use PostgreSQL or MySQL)
Frequent point updates — ClickHouse is append-optimized; mutations (UPDATE/DELETE) are slow and asynchronous
Small datasets where simpler tools (PostgreSQL, SQLite) suffice — ClickHouse has significant setup overhead
Teams needing simple SQL without understanding columnar database concepts like MergeTree engine families
best_when: You need to run fast analytical queries over large volumes of immutable or append-only event/log data, especially when time-series patterns, aggregations over billions of rows, or real-time ingestion are requirements.
avoid_when: You need frequent row-level updates, complex multi-table transactions, or a general-purpose OLTP database.

alternatives.reason: Elasticsearch is better for full-text search and log exploration; ClickHouse is faster for pure aggregation analytics
Neon/PostgreSQL better for OLTP workloads requiring transactions and updates
BigQuery is better for ad-hoc serverless analytics on petabyte-scale datasets in GCP

auth.notes: HTTP interface supports three auth methods: HTTP Basic Auth (user:password), URL parameters (?user=x&password=y), or custom headers (X-ClickHouse-User, X-ClickHouse-Key). ClickHouse Cloud Management API uses a separate API key (Bearer token) created in the Cloud console. Database users can be created with fine-grained privilege grants (SELECT on specific databases/tables). Default 'default' user with empty password is dangerous in production — always configure a password.

pricing.free_tier_limits: ClickHouse Cloud: 30-day free trial with $300 credits. Open-source self-hosted version is free under Apache 2.0.
pricing.paid_tiers: ClickHouse Cloud Development from $0.20/compute-unit-hour + $0.023/GB/month storage. Production tier with multi-replica from ~$250/month minimum. Enterprise: custom.
pricing.notes: Trial doesn't require credit card. Pay-as-you-go after trial. Self-hosted Apache 2.0 open source is completely free — many teams run ClickHouse on-prem for cost control. ClickHouse Cloud auto-scales compute and supports service-level configurations.

performance.rate_limits: No hard rate limits on self-hosted; ClickHouse Cloud scales with cluster configuration. Concurrent query limits configurable per user.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SQL error messages are among the best in any database — they include the exact query position, the problematic token, and often a suggestion. HTTP interface error responses include Exception, Code (ClickHouse error code), and a human-readable message. CRITICAL GOTCHA: HTTP 200 responses do NOT guarantee successful queries — errors can appear mid-stream in the response body; agents must parse response body for 'Exception:' prefix even on 200 status.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Standard INSERT appends data — not idempotent. ReplacingMergeTree engine provides eventual deduplication using version columns, but duplicates may exist temporarily until merges complete. INSERT with deduplication (insert_deduplicate=1, default on ClickHouse Cloud) is idempotent for identical payloads within a deduplication window. DDL operations (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS) are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: HTTP 200 does NOT mean query success — ClickHouse can return a 200 with 'Exception: ...' in the body when errors occur mid-stream; agents MUST check response body for errors
UPDATE and DELETE are mutations — they run asynchronously and may take minutes on large tables; use ReplacingMergeTree/CollapsingMergeTree engine instead of frequent mutations
Eventual deduplication: ReplacingMergeTree deduplicates only at merge time — duplicate rows may exist for minutes to hours after INSERT; COUNT queries may return inflated results
Format must be explicitly specified — default TabSeparated format is not JSON; agents must add FORMAT JSON or FORMAT JSONEachRow to queries or use X-ClickHouse-Format header
Cloud Management API and HTTP query API are entirely separate with different auth credentials — platform token for management, user/password for queries
Multi-statement queries are not supported in a single HTTP request — each query must be a separate HTTP call
Large result sets should use streaming (enable_http_compression=1, wait_end_of_query=0) to avoid OOM — never pull millions of rows into agent memory at once
Dictionary and Materialized View setup is required for many performance optimization patterns — ad-hoc queries on non-partitioned, non-sorted data may be slow despite ClickHouse's speed reputation

security_score_components.security_notes: Username/password + optional TLS. Role-based access control with row and column-level security. ClickHouse Cloud enforces TLS. Self-hosted requires explicit TLS configuration. Excellent for agent analytics workloads due to OLAP optimization.
